title: Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity — TLS Policy Downgrades

app: Splunk Add-on for Stream (Splunkbase 1809) or certificate inventory
spl:
index=net sourcetype=stream:tls earliest=-7d version IN ("SSLv3","TLS1.0","TLS1.1")
| stats dc(src_ip) as clients, values(cipher) as ciphers by dest_ip, dest_port
| sort - clients
description: Detects servers offering weak TLS versions or cipher suites for SC-8 transmission confidentiality and integrity.

grandmaExplanation: We catch servers offering weak secure connections versions or cipher suites for SC-8 transmission confidentiality and integrity — so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for Stream (Splunkbase 1809) or certificate inventory.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `stream:tls` or `tenable:` plugin SSL findings.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Enrich with asset owner; (2) emergency exceptions with expiry; (3) track remediation in RA-7; (4) validate after patch window.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=net sourcetype=stream:tls earliest=-7d version IN ("SSLv3","TLS1.0","TLS1.1")
| stats dc(src_ip) as clients, values(cipher) as ciphers by dest_ip, dest_port
| sort - clients
```

Understanding this SPL

**Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity — TLS Policy Downgrades (SC-8)** — Detects servers offering weak TLS versions or cipher suites for SC-8 transmission confidentiality and integrity.

Documented **Data sources**: `stream:tls` or `tenable:` plugin SSL findings. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Stream (Splunkbase 1809) or certificate inventory. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: net; **sourcetype**: stream:tls. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=net, sourcetype=stream:tls, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by dest_ip, dest_port** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Certificates.All_Certificates by All_Certificates.dest | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity — TLS Policy Downgrades (SC-8)** — Detects servers offering weak TLS versions or cipher suites for SC-8 transmission confidentiality and integrity.

Documented **Data sources**: `stream:tls` or `tenable:` plugin SSL findings. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Stream (Splunkbase 1809) or certificate inventory. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Certificates.All_Certificates` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (weak TLS services), Bar chart (by cipher), Single value (affected IPs)
